methodology_sections: 1. Lifecycle Integrity - State transitions, illegal states, predecessors
2. Monotonicity & Irreversibility - Increasing values, one-way transitions
3. Access & Permission Logic - Conditional access, role requirements
4. Time & Resource Integrity - Time-locks, resource bounds, expiry

invariants.description: _addCredIdPerAddress and _removeCredIdPerAddress are declared public instead of internal, allowing anyone to corrupt per-address cred tracking data structures for any user
invariants.attack_scenario: Attacker calls _removeCredIdPerAddress(credId, victim) to remove a credId from the victim's tracking array. The _credIdsPerAddressCredIdIndex mapping is deleted for that credId. When the victim later tries to sell their last share, _updateCuratorShareBalance calls _removeCredIdPerAddress again, but the index is now stale (default 0), causing WrongCredId revert. Victim's shares and underlying ETH are permanently locked.
invariants.invariant_logic: _addCredIdPerAddress and _removeCredIdPerAddress MUST only be callable by internal contract functions (buy/sell trade execution), never by external accounts
invariants.solidity_property: function invariant_addRemoveCredIdInternal() public { // These functions should not be externally callable // _addCredIdPerAddress and _removeCredIdPerAddress should be internal }

invariants.description: credMerkleRoot is keyed by (credChainId, credId) and shared across all arts for the same cred. Creating a new art for an existing credId overwrites the merkle root, potentially invalidating existing Merkle claims for previously created arts
invariants.attack_scenario: Art A is created for credId=1 with merkleRoot=R1. Users with valid proofs against R1 can claim Art A. Later, Art B is created for credId=1 with merkleRoot=R2, overwriting credMerkleRoot[chainId][1] to R2. Now existing valid minters for Art A whose addresses are in R1 but not R2 can no longer claim Art A. A malicious creator could weaponize this to selectively exclude minters.
invariants.invariant_logic: forall artId: if arts[artId].verificationType == MERKLE, then credMerkleRoot[arts[artId].credChainId][arts[artId].credId] must contain the root that was set when artId was created, OR each art must store its own merkle root
invariants.solidity_property: function invariant_merkleRootStability(uint256 artId) public view { // assert(credMerkleRoot[art.credChainId][art.credId] == originalRootAtCreation[artId]); }

invariants.description: The claim() function forwards only the calculated mintFee to the internal signatureClaim/merkleClaim call via this.call{value: mintFee}, trapping any excess ETH sent by the user in the PhiFactory contract with no refund path to the user
invariants.attack_scenario: User calls claim() with msg.value = 1 ETH but the actual mintFee is 0.001 ETH. The function calls this.merkleClaim{value: 0.001 ether}(...), leaving 0.999 ETH in PhiFactory. The _processClaim refund logic only sees msg.value for the inner call (0.001 ETH) so no refund is issued. The owner can extract stuck ETH via withdraw() to protocolFeeDestination.
invariants.invariant_logic: After claim() executes, msg.value - actualMintFee must be refunded to the original caller, not retained by the contract
invariants.solidity_property: function invariant_claimRefund() public { // assert(address(this).balance_after_claim == address(this).balance_before_claim + mintFee); }

invariants.description: Soulbound status is queried dynamically from PhiFactory art settings via soulBounded(id_), meaning the artist can toggle soulbound status after tokens have been minted, retroactively locking or unlocking already-distributed tokens
invariants.attack_scenario: Artist creates an art with soulBounded=false. Users mint and expect to trade on secondary markets. Artist later calls updateArtSettings with soulBounded=true, making all previously minted tokens non-transferable. Alternatively, a soulbound credential could be made transferable, undermining the credential's integrity.
invariants.invariant_logic: once soulBounded status is set for an art with numberMinted > 0, it SHOULD NOT change, OR soulbound status should be snapshot at mint time per token
invariants.solidity_property: function invariant_soulboundImmutable(uint256 artId) public view { // if (arts[artId].numberMinted > 0) assert(arts[artId].soulBounded == originalSoulbound[artId]); }

invariants.description: The distribute() function sends the royalty fee plus rounding dust to msg.sender (the caller), allowing any external account to call distribute and claim the royalty fee as a reward, creating an MEV/front-running incentive
invariants.attack_scenario: A large curator reward deposit is made. A bot front-runs legitimate distribution calls to claim the 1% royalty fee. With large balances, this could be substantial. The distributor also lacks reentrancy protection, and the ETH transfer to msg.sender happens before the depositBatch call to phiRewards.
invariants.invariant_logic: royaltyfee from distribute() SHOULD only be claimable by an authorized party OR the royalty fee extraction should happen through a controlled mechanism, not freely by any caller
invariants.solidity_property: function invariant_distributeAccessControl() public { // Only authorized callers should receive the royalty fee from distribute() }

invariants.description: Single buy/sell trades via _handleTrade lack nonReentrant protection. The function makes multiple external calls (bonding curve, safeTransferETH to seller/sender, protocolFee transfer, phiRewards deposit) after state updates. While state is updated before transfers, the absence of reentrancy guards combined with multiple external calls creates risk.
invariants.invariant_logic: During execution of _handleTrade, no reentrant call to any Cred state-changing function should be possible

invariants.description: _getCreatorFee does not return early when supply_ == 0, unlike getPriceData which correctly returns creatorFee=0. The if-block sets creatorFee=0 but lacks a return statement, so execution continues and overwrites creatorFee with a non-zero value. This causes getBuyPriceAfterFee and getSellPriceAfterFee to return incorrect prices when supply is 0.
invariants.attack_scenario: When supply is 0 (first share purchase or all shares sold), getBuyPriceAfterFee includes a creator fee that is not actually charged during the real trade (which uses getPriceData). Users relying on getBuyPriceAfterFee see an inflated price and may overpay or avoid purchasing. Front-ends displaying wrong prices erode trust.
invariants.invariant_logic: forall credId, amount: getBuyPriceAfterFee(credId, supply, amount) == getPriceData(credId, supply, amount, true).price + getPriceData(...).protocolFee + getPriceData(...).creatorFee
invariants.solidity_property: function invariant_priceConsistency(uint256 credId, uint256 supply, uint256 amount) public view { (uint256 p, uint256 pf, uint256 cf) = bondingCurve.getPriceData(credId, supply, amount, true); assert(bondingCurve.getBuyPriceAfterFee(credId, supply, amount) == p + pf + cf); }

invariants.description: Cred.setProtocolFeePercent has no upper bound check. The owner can set protocolFeePercent to any value including >10000 (>100%). If protocolFee + creatorFee exceed the price, sells become impossible because price - protocolFee - creatorFee underflows, permanently trapping curator funds.
invariants.invariant_logic: protocolFeePercent + max(buyShareRoyalty, sellShareRoyalty) < RATIO_BASE, ensuring fees never exceed the trade price
invariants.solidity_property: function invariant_feesBounded() public view { assert(protocolFeePercent < 10000); // and combined with royalties < 10000 }

invariants.description: getPositionsForCurator has an indexing bug: it writes to credIds[i] and amounts[i] instead of credIds[index] and amounts[index]. When start_ > 0 or entries are skipped due to _credIdExistsPerAddress being false, the written values end up at wrong array positions, and the assembly truncation discards the actual data.
invariants.attack_scenario: Off-chain systems relying on getPositionsForCurator receive arrays with zeros where valid data should be, or miss valid positions entirely. This corrupts portfolio display and could lead to incorrect decisions in automated systems.
invariants.invariant_logic: forall i in [0, result.length): credIds[i] != 0 && amounts[i] == shareBalance[credIds[i]].get(curator_)
invariants.solidity_property: function invariant_positionsCorrect(address curator) public view { (uint256[] memory ids, uint256[] memory amts) = getPositionsForCurator(curator, 0, 0); for (uint i = 0; i < ids.length; i++) { assert(ids[i] != 0); } }

invariants.description: withdrawFor(from, amount) allows ANY external caller to trigger a withdrawal of 'amount' from 'from' balance, sending ETH to 'from'. With amount=0 (FULL_BALANCE sentinel), anyone can force-withdraw a user's entire balance to the user's address without their consent.
invariants.invariant_logic: Withdrawal from an account SHOULD require authorization from the account owner (msg.sender == from, or valid signature via withdrawWithSig)
invariants.solidity_property: function invariant_withdrawRequiresAuth() public { // withdrawFor should verify msg.sender is authorized to withdraw on behalf of from }

invariants.description: PhiNFT1155 inherits UUPSUpgradeable with _authorizeUpgrade restricted to onlyOwner (PhiFactory). However, PhiFactory does not expose any function to call upgradeToAndCall on deployed PhiNFT1155 clones. The NFT contracts are effectively non-upgradeable despite being designed as upgradeable.
invariants.attack_scenario: A critical vulnerability is discovered in PhiNFT1155 (e.g., in mint logic or transfer logic). Since PhiFactory cannot trigger an upgrade on the NFT contracts, the vulnerability cannot be patched. All existing credential NFTs are permanently at risk.
invariants.invariant_logic: If a contract inherits UUPSUpgradeable, the authorized upgrader MUST have a reachable code path to invoke upgradeToAndCall
invariants.solidity_property: function invariant_upgradeReachable() public { // PhiFactory must expose a function that calls IPhiNFT1155(artAddress).upgradeToAndCall(...) }

invariants.description: distribute() iterates over ALL curator addresses (getCuratorAddresses with stop_=0) and calls getShareNumber for each. For popular creds with hundreds or thousands of curators, this can exceed the block gas limit, making distribution permanently impossible and locking curator rewards.
invariants.invariant_logic: Distribution must be feasible for any number of curators; either pagination support or a pull-based mechanism is needed to ensure balanceOf[credId] is always eventually distributable
invariants.solidity_property: function invariant_distributeFeasible(uint256 credId) public view { // assert(credContract.getCuratorAddressLength(credId) <= MAX_DISTRIBUTABLE); }

invariants.description: The share lock period is enforced in _handleTrade for single sells and in _executeBatchTrade for batch sells, but the batch buy path in _executeBatchTrade sets lastTradeTimestamp inside the loop. For batch buys, the lock period is correctly set. However, when createCred calls buyShareCred internally, the creator's lock timestamp is set, preventing immediate sell of the initial share, which is correct.
invariants.invariant_logic: forall (credId, curator): if lastTradeTimestamp[credId][curator] > 0, then sell of credId by curator MUST revert if block.timestamp <= lastTradeTimestamp[credId][curator] + SHARE_LOCK_PERIOD
invariants.solidity_property: function invariant_lockPeriod(uint256 credId, address curator) public view { if (lastTradeTimestamp[credId][curator] > 0) { // sell must revert if block.timestamp <= lastTradeTimestamp[credId][curator] + SHARE_LOCK_PERIOD } }

invariants.description: The supply invariant that currentSupply equals the sum of all share balances must hold at all times. Since _addCredIdPerAddress and _removeCredIdPerAddress are public, external calls can desynchronize the tracking arrays from actual balances without affecting currentSupply or shareBalance, but could cause _updateCuratorShareBalance to revert when it tries to re-add or re-remove entries.
invariants.invariant_logic: forall credId: creds[credId].currentSupply == sum(shareBalance[credId].values()) AND the tracking arrays must be consistent with shareBalance
invariants.solidity_property: function invariant_supplyBalanceConsistency(uint256 credId) public view { uint256 total = 0; uint256 len = shareBalance[credId].length(); for (uint i = 0; i < len; i++) { (, uint256 val) = shareBalance[credId].at(i); total += val; } assert(creds[credId].currentSupply == total); }

invariants.description: handleRewardsAndGetValueSent is external payable with no access control. While callers must pay the full mint reward in msg.value, anyone can call it to deposit rewards for arbitrary addresses and emit RewardsDeposit events, potentially polluting event logs used for off-chain indexing.
invariants.invariant_logic: handleRewardsAndGetValueSent SHOULD only be callable by authorized PhiNFT1155 contracts to ensure reward events correspond to actual mints
